Of the fifteen fastest-moving economies, `r n_europe` of `r n_total` are in Europe, confirming that the momentum ranking is not evenly spread but concentrated in a specific policy environment; consistent with EU-level renewable targets and national feed-in-tariff schemes rather than a generalised global trend. The three fastest movers are `r paste(top3, collapse = ", ")`.

A bar chart of endpoint change, however, cannot distinguish a country that climbed steadily across fifteen years from one that surged late and plateaued; a distinction the report has treated as analytically important since the shock-recovery curves in @sec-shocks. The slope graph below adds that missing dimension in static form.

```{r q5-slope}
#| label: fig-q5-slope
#| fig-cap: "Renewable share of primary energy, 2008 versus 2023, for the fifteen fastest-moving economies. The slope of each line is the pace of change; countries are ordered and coloured by their 2023 rank, so the steepest lines identify the countries that not only gained the most but did so most consistently across the period."
#| fig-height: 6.5

slope_df <- change_tbl |>
  select(country, starts_with("yr_"), change_pp) |>
  arrange(desc(change_pp)) |>
  mutate(rank = row_number()) |>
  pivot_longer(starts_with("yr_"), names_to = "yr", values_to = "share") |>
  mutate(year = if_else(yr == paste0("yr_", start_yr), start_yr, end_yr))

ggplot(slope_df, aes(year, share, group = country)) +
  geom_line(aes(colour = rank), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(aes(colour = rank), size = 2) +
  geom_text_repel(data = filter(slope_df, year == start_yr),
                  aes(label = country), hjust = 1, nudge_x = -1.5,
                  size = 2.9, direction = "y", segment.size = 0.2) +
  geom_text_repel(data = filter(slope_df, year == end_yr),
                  aes(label = country), hjust = 0, nudge_x = 1.5,
                  size = 2.9, direction = "y", segment.size = 0.2) +
  scale_colour_viridis_c(option = "viridis", guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(start_yr, end_yr),
                     limits = c(start_yr - 6, end_yr + 6)) +
  labs(title = "Steady climbers versus late sprinters",
       subtitle = paste0("Renewable share of primary energy, ", start_yr,
                          " and ", end_yr),
       x = NULL, y = "Renewables share of primary energy (%)",
       caption = owid_caption)
```

## The path, not just the endpoint {#sec-scoreboard-animated}

The animation below plays the same fifteen countries' renewable share forward year by year across the full `r start_yr`–`r end_yr` window, making the *shape* of each trajectory; steady, accelerating, or plateauing, directly visible rather than inferred from two endpoints.

```{r q5-animated}
#| label: fig-q5-animated
#| fig-cap: !expr paste0("Animated trajectories of renewable share, ", start_yr, " to ", end_yr, ", for the fifteen fastest-moving economies. Each frame is one year; lines trace each country's full path rather than only its start and end point.")
#| fig-height: 6
#| eval: !expr knitr::is_html_output()

# Manual frame-by-frame renderer using magick instead of gganimate/gifski.
# gganimate's internal frame queue occasionally hangs on some Apple Silicon
# R setups with no error message; this version renders one ggsave() per year
# (each printing a visible progress message, so you can see it is actually
# working) and stitches the PNGs into a gif with magick, which uses a
# completely separate code path. Requires: install.packages("magick").
# eval only for HTML output, since the PDF/Word targets cannot embed a gif.

leader_countries <- change_tbl$country

traj_df <- energy |>
  filter(country %in% leader_countries, year >= start_yr, year <= end_yr,
         !is.na(renewables_share_energy)) |>
  select(country, year, share = renewables_share_energy) |>
  left_join(change_tbl |> select(country, change_pp), by = "country") |>
  mutate(country = fct_reorder(country, change_pp))

y_max <- max(traj_df$share, na.rm = TRUE) * 1.05

frame_dir <- "gif-cache/q5-frames"
dir.create(frame_dir, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)

years_seq   <- seq(start_yr, end_yr)
frame_files <- character(length(years_seq))

for (i in seq_along(years_seq)) {
  yr <- years_seq[i]
  message("Rendering frame ", i, " of ", length(years_seq), " (year ", yr, ")")

  df_frame     <- traj_df |> filter(year <= yr)
  labels_frame <- df_frame |> group_by(country) |>
    filter(year == max(year)) |> ungroup()

  p_frame <- ggplot(df_frame, aes(year, share, colour = country, group = country)) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
    geom_point(data = labels_frame, size = 2.4) +
    geom_text(data = labels_frame, aes(label = country), hjust = -0.05,
              size = 3.4, fontface = "bold") +
    scale_colour_viridis_d(option = "viridis", guide = "none") +
    scale_x_continuous(limits = c(start_yr, end_yr + 14),
                       breaks = seq(start_yr, end_yr, 3)) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, y_max)) +
    labs(title = paste0("Renewable share of primary energy: ", yr),
         subtitle = "Fifteen fastest-moving economies, animated by year",
         x = NULL, y = "Renewables share of primary energy (%)",
         caption = owid_caption) +
    coord_cartesian(clip = "off") +
    theme(plot.margin = margin(5.5, 90, 5.5, 5.5))

  fpath <- file.path(frame_dir, sprintf("frame_%03d.png", i))
  ggsave(fpath, p_frame, width = 8, height = 5.5, dpi = 100)
  frame_files[i] <- fpath
}

message("Stitching ", length(frame_files), " frames into gif...")
gif_out <- image_animate(image_join(image_read(frame_files)), fps = 5, loop = 0)
gif_path <- file.path(frame_dir, "renewable_trajectory.gif")
image_write(gif_out, path = gif_path)

knitr::include_graphics(gif_path)
```

Read together with the map in @sec-map, these three figures reinforce the same conclusion from three angles: the leaders are concentrated in Western Europe, their gains are recent rather than gradual across the whole period, and; visible only in the animated view; several show the acceleration late in the window that a simple two-point comparison would understate. The appropriate criterion of leadership is therefore the rate of change, and specifically its recent trajectory, which identifies the economies driving the contemporary transition rather than those favoured by geographical endowment.

# Discussion and Conclusion {#sec-conclusion}

Returning to the question posed at the outset; how, where, and why the world decarbonises unevenly, the analysis converges on a single answer: unevenness is driven by policy-mediated differences in energy-system composition, not by differences in wealth, and it is increasingly geographically concentrated even as global fossil demand keeps rising in absolute terms.

## Summary of findings {#sec-conclusion-summary}

```{r conclusion-summary-table}
#| label: tbl-conclusion-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Headline figures underlying the report's conclusions, drawn from the sections indicated."

summary_headline <- tibble(
  Finding = c(
    "Global energy demand growth, 1990–2024",
    "Correlation, GDP per capita vs renewable share",
    "Asia's share of global CO2, 2022",
    "Fastest-moving economies concentrated in Europe",
    "France's carbon-intensity change, 1973–2000"
  ),
  Value = c(
    "Rising throughout; fossil bands did not contract (§4.1)",
    sprintf("r = %.2f (near zero) (§2.3)", r_gdp_ren),
    "Majority of global total, up from a small share at mid-century (§6.1)",
    sprintf("%d of %d fastest-moving economies (§7)", n_europe, n_total),
    sprintf("%s%% (§5.1)",
            if (exists("fr_drop") && length(fr_drop)) sprintf("%+.0f", fr_drop) else "~-40")
  )
)

knitr::kable(summary_headline, col.names = c("Finding", "Headline figure"))
```

At the global scale, renewable capacity has so far been additive rather than substitutive, demand continues to rise, and external shocks dent but do not redirect the emissions curve, indicating that emissions are governed by the structural composition of energy systems rather than by transient events. At the national scale, however, prosperity and emissions can be separated, and the decisive factor is the manner in which energy is produced rather than the quantity of wealth a country commands. The comparative study of the oil crises and the COVID-19 pandemic suggests that the openings created by shocks tend to be realised most fully where policy acts upon them, and that renewable generation, once installed, is structurally insulated from demand shocks. The spatial analysis demonstrates that the emissions centre of gravity has shifted eastward even as several Western economies separated wealth from carbon, and that the spread of renewables comprises two distinct phases, an older hydroelectric one and a newer wind-and-solar one. When leadership is measured by momentum rather than inherited position, as in @sec-scoreboard, the economies advancing fastest are concentrated in a specific policy environment rather than distributed evenly across income levels or geography. The overall conclusion is that the transition is accelerating and that wealth and carbon can in principle be separated, yet absolute global fossil-fuel use remains persistently high.

## Limitations {#sec-conclusion-limitations}

Several limitations qualify these findings. OWID compiles self-reported national statistics of varying quality and timeliness, so the most recent years are frequently incomplete; the GDP series in particular derives from the Maddison Project Database, whose latest observations are sparse, which is why the cross-sectional reference year is selected for joint coverage rather than fixed at the final year of the emissions series. Sparse coverage before 1990 likewise means that comparisons involving the earlier period rest on a thinner sample, and strong cross-country claims are accordingly restricted to the well-covered era. Per-capita ratios are unstable for very small populations, which are excluded from the relevant figures. The analysis relies throughout on territorial emissions, whereas a fuller account would foreground consumption-based emissions to capture the carbon embodied in trade, which flatters net-importing economies. Finally, the indicator of renewable share aggregates established hydroelectric capacity with newer wind and solar generation, a conflation addressed only partially through the momentum ranking of @sec-scoreboard.

## Future work {#sec-conclusion-future}

Were the analysis to be repeated, three refinements would be prioritised. Decoupling would be expressed as a formal quantity, namely the elasticity of CO2 with respect to GDP, rather than conveyed through visual impression; consumption-based emissions would be incorporated to account for carbon embodied in imports; and a single analysis year would be standardised across all figures to facilitate cross-chart comparison, an aim already partly served here by the automatically selected reference year. Methodologically, the project reinforced two lessons. The greater part of the effort was devoted to data preparation rather than to plotting, and the most consequential single safeguard was the consistent removal of OWID's aggregate rows, since a careless join silently double-counts. The exploratory analysis, moreover, proved not to be a preliminary exercise but the source of the report's central question, with the near-zero correlation between wealth and renewable share directing the subsequent investigation.



```{r session}
#| label: session-info
#| code-fold: show
sessionInfo()
```
